subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Type Ia Supernovae: Standard Candles
description: A 60-second lesson on Type Ia Supernovae: Standard Candles.

hook.text: A white dwarf steals gas from a companion star until it hits a critical mass—and detonates completely.

buildup.text: Because the explosion always happens at the same mass, every Type Ia supernova has nearly the same brightness.

discovery.text: Astronomers use them as 'standard candles'—if you know the true brightness, you can calculate the distance.

twist.text: In 1998, Type Ia supernovae revealed that the universe's expansion is accelerating—driven by dark energy.

climax.text: Saul Perlmutter, Brian Schmidt, and Adam Riess won the 2011 Nobel Prize for this discovery.

punchline.text: Exploding white dwarfs revealed dark energy. Dead stars lit the way.

quiz.question: How did Type Ia supernovae help discover dark energy?
options: Their consistent brightness allowed measuring distances that showed the universe is accelerating
They emitted dark energy particles detected by ground-based labs
Their explosions slowed down over time, revealing a hidden force
